This level of accuracy, given casually in the middle of a narrative about faith and materialism, is yet another quiet miracle of the Quran. The Prophet ﷺ, who could neither read nor write, who had no access to astronomical calculations, delivers this information as if it’s common knowledge. But it’s not common knowledge - it’s revealed knowledge, precise and true.

This detail closes the story with a reminder that every element of this narrative, down to the exact duration of the young men’s sleep, comes from the One who controls time itself. The same Allah ﷻ who kept these young men alive for three centuries is the One who will resurrect all of humanity on the Day of Judgment. Their story stands as an eternal sign of His power, His mercy, and His promise that those who sacrifice for His sake will never be forgotten.

Ayah 26–29 · linked video: 06. Al-Kahf (Ayah 26-29)

Surah Al-Kahf: Ayahs 26-29

Ayah 26

قُلِ ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا لَبِثُوا۟ ۖ لَهُۥ غَيْبُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ أَبْصِرْ بِهِۦ وَأَسْمِعْ ۚ مَا لَهُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا يُشْرِكُ فِى حُكْمِهِۦٓ أَحَدًا

Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ: “Tell them, Allah knows better about how long they stayed.” The focus shifts from the debates about duration to a powerful declaration of Allah’s exclusive knowledge of the unseen. He alone owns the unseen of the heavens and the earth—no one else has access to this knowledge.

The ayah then presents a remarkable phrase: أَبْصِرْ بِهِۦ وَأَسْمِعْ (observe how amazingly well He sees, and how amazingly well He hears). This is one of the rare places in the Quran where seeing is mentioned before hearing. The significance relates directly to the story of the cave—these young believers were hidden behind multiple barriers where no human eye could discover them, yet Allah ﷻ saw them perfectly. Even their most private conversations, their whispered plans about getting food and avoiding detection, were all within His knowledge. The Quran shares only highlights of their discussions, teaching us that whatever Allah ﷻ chose to reveal must contain lessons for us to extract and understand.

The ayah concludes with a powerful statement: مَا لَهُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ مِن وَلِىٍّ (they have no protective friend besides Him), وَلَا يُشْرِكُ فِى حُكْمِهِۦٓ أَحَدًا (and He allows no one whatsoever to share in His rule). This establishes Allah’s absolute authority—there is no partner in His judgment and no rival to His power.

Ayah 27

وَٱتْلُ مَآ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْكَ مِن كِتَابِ رَبِّكَ ۖ لَا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦ وَلَن تَجِدَ مِن دُونِهِۦ مُلْتَحَدًا

Having addressed the speculations about the People of the Cave, Allah ﷻ now instructs His Messenger ﷺ: “Keep reciting what has been revealed to you from the book of your Master.” This command addresses the pressure the Prophet ﷺ faced from the Jewish scholars of Madinah who questioned him about various historical details. They would ask question after question, trying to dictate what topics he should address.

The instruction is clear: don’t allow them to determine your message. The Quran will tell you what to discuss. When they claim the story doesn’t impress them or doesn’t match their expectations, the response is definitive: لَا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦ (no one can change His words). What has been revealed is perfect and complete. This carries a sharp criticism of how previous nations altered Allah’s revelation, but also a promise—this final revelation will remain protected and unchanged.

The ayah ends with وَلَن تَجِدَ مِن دُونِهِۦ مُلْتَحَدًا (you will not find any refuge besides Him). The word مُلْتَحَدًا refers to a space with enough room to wiggle out, like loosely tied ropes or prison bars with gaps wide enough to slip through. The message is absolute: there is no escape route, no alternative path, no wiggle room away from Allah’s revelation and judgment.

Ayah 28

وَٱصْبِرْ نَفْسَكَ مَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَدَوٰةِ وَٱلْعَشِىِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُۥ ۖ وَلَا تَعْدُ عَيْنَاكَ عَنْهُمْ تُرِيدُ زِينَةَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَا تُطِعْ مَنْ أَغْفَلْنَا قَلْبَهُۥ عَن ذِكْرِنَا وَٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ وَكَانَ أَمْرُهُۥ فُرُطًا

Having addressed the intellectual elite—the Jewish scholars—Allah ﷻ now addresses the challenge of the social elite, the leaders of Quraysh. The Prophet ﷺ faced pressure from two distinct groups: intellectual elites who made him feel his message wasn’t sophisticated enough, and social elites who wanted private audiences without the “lower class” companions around him.

The command وَٱصْبِرْ نَفْسَكَ (make yourself patient) literally means “hold yourself back, restrain yourself.” Be patient and content with those who call upon their Master بِٱلْغَدَوٰةِ وَٱلْعَشِىِّ (in the late morning and early evening). These terms—Al-Ghadwah and Al-’Ashiy—refer to the busiest hours of the day, the time when markets open and business is conducted. These are the hours when most people are hustling to make their livelihood. Yet these believers, instead of chasing worldly gain, openly and courageously call upon their Lord during these public hours.

They want nothing except يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُۥ (His face)—meaning His pleasure, the honor of being addressed by Him on the Day of Judgment, the light that will illuminate their faces when they meet their Creator. These are people who have left everything for the sake of Allah ﷻ.

The instruction continues: وَلَا تَعْدُ عَيْنَاكَ عَنْهُمْ (don’t let your eyes cross beyond them). The word تَعْدُ means to cross a line or fence. It’s as if the Sahaba form a boundary, and Allah ﷻ is telling His Prophet ﷺ: don’t let your eyes look past this fence hoping for someone else—someone wealthier, more influential, more politically powerful.

This addresses a crucial leadership principle. The Prophet ﷺ naturally wanted influential people to embrace Islam because their conversion could strengthen the Muslim community. If a leader of Quraysh accepted Islam, it would empower the message. If a Jewish rabbi converted, he might bring his entire congregation. Some people carry more social, political and economic weight than others—this is simply a fact.

But here’s the revolutionary aspect of Islam’s message: when it comes to spiritual value and standing before Allah ﷻ, all hierarchies are eliminated. The only measure that matters is taqwa. If the Prophet ﷺ gave even the impression that some people deserved more of his time because of their worldly status, it would undermine this foundational teaching. The weaker companions would sense that despite the message of equality, the old social hierarchies still held some weight.

The rhetorical question cuts deep: تُرِيدُ زِينَةَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا (Do you want the beauty of worldly life?) The Prophet ﷺ didn’t want personal power or luxury—he wanted Islam to flourish, he wanted Allah’s word to be supreme. Yet even this noble intention, when it leads to prioritizing the influential over the sincere believers, is characterized as seeking worldly beauty. This shows the extraordinarily high standard expected of the Messenger ﷺ. What would be praiseworthy for us is considered inadequate for him because of his elevated station.

The ayah concludes with a warning: وَلَا تُطِعْ مَنْ أَغْفَلْنَا قَلْبَهُۥ عَن ذِكْرِنَا (don’t follow or be influenced by those whose hearts We have emptied of Our remembrance). These are people whose hearts were so corrupted that they didn’t deserve Allah’s mention, so He removed all remembrance of Himself from their hearts. What remains? وَٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ (He follows his desires)—empty desires that lead him further astray. وَكَانَ أَمْرُهُۥ فُرُطًا (Everything he does is in excess)—excessive evil, excessive criticism, going overboard in transgression.

The message is clear: those influential people seeking to negotiate terms with Islam aren’t worth the compromise. Compare them to the believers who want nothing but Allah’s face, and the choice becomes obvious.

Ayah 29

وَقُلِ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ فَمَن شَآءَ فَلْيُؤْمِن وَمَن شَآءَ فَلْيَكْفُرْ ۚ إِنَّآ أَعْتَدْنَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ نَارًا أَحَاطَ بِهِمْ سُرَادِقُهَا ۚ وَإِن يَسْتَغِيثُوا۟ يُغَاثُوا۟ بِمَآءٍ كَٱلْمُهْلِ يَشْوِى ٱلْوُجُوهَ ۚ بِئْسَ ٱلشَّرَابُ وَسَآءَتْ مُرْتَفَقًا

Now comes the decisive declaration: وَقُلِ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ (Tell them the truth is from your Master). There’s no need to package it differently for the elite or water it down for skeptics. Present it plainly: فَمَن شَآءَ فَلْيُؤْمِن وَمَن شَآءَ فَلْيَكْفُرْ (whoever wants to believe, let them believe; whoever wants to reject, let them reject).

This stark choice carries shock value. It communicates that the Prophet ﷺ isn’t desperate for their acceptance. Don’t give them the impression that you need to convince them or that Islam requires their validation. They can take it or leave it.

Think of a desperate salesman watching his customer walk away, offering discounts and bonuses to make them stay. If the Prophet ﷺ were a desperate salesman, he might sweeten the deal as they leave. But instead of incentives, he offers a warning—and it’s one of the most vivid descriptions of punishment in the Quran.

إِنَّآ أَعْتَدْنَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ نَارًا (We have already prepared for the wrongdoers a fire). The past tense is significant—the fire isn’t something that will be prepared; it already exists. أَحَاطَ بِهِمْ سُرَادِقُهَا (Its walls have already surrounded them). The word سُرَادِق—borrowed into Arabic from Persian—refers to a tent structure without a roof, the kind used for large gatherings. This detail becomes crucial in understanding what follows.

وَإِن يَسْتَغِيثُوا۟ (When they cry out for relief), specifically asking for rain to extinguish the flames, يُغَاثُوا۟ بِمَآءٍ كَٱلْمُهْلِ (they will be given water like molten metal). The word مُهْل refers to the disgusting yellow pus that oozes from a corpse, or the molten mixture of fire and metal that flows in foundries—that golden, terrifying liquid. It describes any liquid that is either utterly repulsive or frighteningly dangerous.

Why was it important that this tent has no roof? So the “rain” can fall directly on them. يَشْوِى ٱلْوُجُوهَ (It roasts their faces). When rain typically falls, people lower their heads for protection. But in this torment, necks are held back, faces forced upward to receive every burning drop. The liquid doesn’t just hit their faces—it continues down their throats.

بِئْسَ ٱلشَّرَابُ (What a horrible drink!)—establishing that this isn’t merely external punishment, but something they’re forced to consume. وَسَآءَتْ مُرْتَفَقًا (What a horrible place to lean back and relax). The word مُرْتَفَقًا refers to a place where one reclines in comfort. The irony is devastating—this is their eternal “resting place.”

This powerful conclusion reminds those who arrogantly reject the message that their choice carries eternal consequences. The truth has been presented clearly, without compromise or desperation. The choice—and its results—are theirs alone.

Ayah 30–37 · linked video: 07. Al-Kahf (Ayah 30-37)

Surah Al-Kahf: Ayahs 30-37

Ayah 30

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ إِنَّا لَا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ مَنْ أَحْسَنَ عَمَلًا

After describing the punishment awaiting the disbelievers, Allah ﷻ now turns our attention to those who believe. The ayah begins with emphasis: “Indeed, those who believed and have done the good deeds asked of them.” This is not merely about claiming faith, but about demonstrating it through righteous action.

Allah ﷻ then makes a promise with absolute certainty: “Without doubt, We will not waste the compensation of the one who excelled in terms of good deeds.” The phrase أَحْسَنَ عَمَلًا refers to those who do their deeds with excellence and beauty. Notice how Allah ﷻ attributes this promise directly to Himself using “We” - this is the royal plural showing His majesty and the certainty of this guarantee. Your good deeds will never be lost or wasted with Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 31

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهِمُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ يُحَلَّوْنَ فِيهَا مِنْ أَسَاوِرَ مِن ذَهَبٍ وَيَلْبَسُونَ ثِيَابًا خُضْرًا مِّن سُندُسٍ وَإِسْتَبْرَقٍ مُّتَّكِـِٔينَ فِيهَا عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ ۚ نِعْمَ ٱلثَّوَابُ وَحَسُنَتْ مُرْتَفَقًا

These are the people who will have جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ - the highest levels of Paradise. Notice the beautiful contrast Allah ﷻ presents: earlier in the surah, the people of the Fire had boiling water poured on them from above as a source of torture. But here, water flows from beneath the believers - from underneath their dwellings, like springs emerging from within their own gardens. The water comes from below as a source of joy and beauty.

The description continues with details that paint a picture of absolute royalty. يُحَلَّوْنَ means they will be adorned and decorated - not that they dress themselves, but rather they are dressed by others, like kings and queens attended by servants. They will wear bracelets of gold. Now, you might think, “I don’t wear jewelry, that would look strange.” But understand that your fashion sense in Jannah will be different. Think about it - you might not wear a crown, but you wear a nice watch or a baseball cap. You have your own sense of style with jackets and nice clothes. Allah ﷻ will take that sense of beauty you already have and elevate it with the finest materials.

They will wear garments that are خُضْرًا - green in color, made from سُندُسٍ - light silk, and إِسْتَبْرَقٍ - heavy silk. Two different textures of the finest silk. The scene depicts them مُّتَّكِـِٔينَ - reclining back on large cushions and couches, dressed magnificently, drinks all around them, completely at ease.

Allah ﷻ concludes this description with نِعْمَ ٱلثَّوَابُ - what an awesome compensation, what an excellent reward! وَحَسُنَتْ مُرْتَفَقًا - and how beautiful it is as a place to find comfort and relaxation. The word مُرْتَفَقًا comes from the same root as رِفْق, meaning a place of ease and rest.

The contrast is stark: one group is held up in the Fire, faces being burned, while the other reclines in comfort, completely at peace.

Ayah 32

۞ وَٱضْرِبْ لَهُم مَّثَلًا رَّجُلَيْنِ جَعَلْنَا لِأَحَدِهِمَا جَنَّتَيْنِ مِنْ أَعْنَـٰبٍ وَحَفَفْنَـٰهُمَا بِنَخْلٍ وَجَعَلْنَا بَيْنَهُمَا زَرْعًا

Having shown us the contrast between Paradise and Hellfire in the afterlife, Allah ﷻ now presents a contrast between two types of people living similar lives in this world. وَٱضْرِبْ لَهُم - “Give them an example” - this is directed to the Prophet ﷺ, but the example is for all of us.

The parable concerns رَّجُلَيْنِ - two men. But notice something remarkable: Allah ﷻ says, “We made for one of them two gardens,” and from this point forward, the entire discussion focuses on only one of the two men. What about the other man? We hear almost nothing about him throughout this passage. This silence itself is significant - the second man’s property was so insignificant that Allah ﷻ doesn’t even bother describing it.

Let’s examine what the first man was given. جَعَلْنَا لِأَحَدِهِمَا جَنَّتَيْنِ - “We set up two gardens for one of them.” Notice Allah ﷻ attributes the setup to Himself. He doesn’t just say the man had two gardens; He says “We set him up with two gardens.” This means everything was perfectly arranged, meticulously planned.

These gardens were مِنْ أَعْنَـٰبٍ - of grapes. Grapes are among the most delicate fruits to cultivate. Vineyards require expensive land, careful maintenance, and represent serious wealth. Think of famous vineyard regions - this is prime real estate.

وَحَفَفْنَـٰهُمَا بِنَخْلٍ - “And We surrounded both of them with palm trees.” The word حَفَفْنَـٰهُمَا means to surround from both sides, to fortify. Why palm trees? They serve as a natural protection system. When wind blows from either direction, the palm trees shield the delicate grapevines from damage. Again, notice how Allah ﷻ doesn’t say the gardens happened to have palm trees around them - He says “We surrounded the gardens with trees.” By attributing this to Himself, Allah ﷻ emphasizes how perfectly arranged everything was. The wealthy should remember this: they didn’t set all this up themselves. Allah ﷻ set it up for them.

وَجَعَلْنَا بَيْنَهُمَا زَرْعًا - “And between both of them We placed crops.” Between the two gardens ran fields of other crops. So this man didn’t just have vineyards - he had diversified agricultural investments, all of them running successfully.

Ayah 33

كِلْتَا ٱلْجَنَّتَيْنِ ءَاتَتْ أُكُلَهَا وَلَمْ تَظْلِم مِّنْهُ شَيْـًٔا ۚ وَفَجَّرْنَا خِلَـٰلَهُمَا نَهَرًا

كِلْتَا is the feminine dual form meaning “both” or “the entirety of both.” It’s not just saying “both gardens” - it’s emphasizing the complete totality. One hundred percent of both gardens ءَاتَتْ أُكُلَهَا - produced their food, their fruits. Not a single tree, not a single branch, not a single inch was unproductive. Everything was yielding perfectly. This level of total productivity makes sense when we remember that Allah ﷻ is the One who set it all up.

وَلَمْ تَظْلِم مِّنْهُ شَيْـًٔا - “And it did not fall short in anything.” The word تَظْلِم is interesting here. It comes from ظلم, which means to wrong someone or to withhold something that is due. This language is typically used for employees. Imagine a cashier who collects money all day and at the end of his shift is supposed to hand it all to the employer, but he keeps a bit for himself - that would be ظلم. By using this word, Allah ﷻ is saying these gardens were like perfect employees that never made a mistake, never held anything back, never fell short in their productivity. The gardens were completely at his service.

وَفَجَّرْنَا خِلَـٰلَهُمَا نَهَرًا - “And We caused a river to burst through between them.” The word فَجَّرْنَا means to explode forth, to burst out. A permanent irrigation system flowed right through both properties, ensuring constant water supply.

Let’s take stock: this man has palm trees providing protection, vineyards producing the most valuable fruits, diversified crops, 100% productivity across all his land, and a river flowing through it all for perfect irrigation. That’s one of the two men. We still haven’t heard what the second man has.

Ayah 34

وَكَانَ لَهُۥ ثَمَرٌ فَقَالَ لِصَـٰحِبِهِۦ وَهُوَ يُحَاوِرُهُۥٓ أَنَا۠ أَكْثَرُ مِنكَ مَالًا وَأَعَزُّ نَفَرًا

وَكَانَ لَهُۥ ثَمَرٌ - “And he had fruit.” Previously, Allah ﷻ mentioned gardens. Now He mentions ثَمَرٌ - fruit, which also means wealth. The word connects both meanings: his gardens produced fruit, and that fruit produced wealth. This man had achieved tremendous material success - 100% productivity translating to maximum profits.

Now comes the human interaction. This wealthy man has a neighbor - his صَاحِب. According to what we can understand from the Quran’s description, this neighbor has such insignificant property that Allah ﷻ gives us no details about it whatsoever. The contrast in their economic status is stark.

فَقَالَ لِصَـٰحِبِهِۦ وَهُوَ يُحَاوِرُهُۥٓ - “And he said to his companion while conversing with him.” The word يُحَاوِرُهُۥٓ means to have a back-and-forth conversation, a dialogue. Notice something crucial: وَهُوَ - HE initiated the conversation. The wealthy man went to his neighbor to talk.

أَنَا۠ أَكْثَرُ مِنكَ مَالًا - “I have more wealth than you.” But here’s what’s important to understand: he didn’t knock on his neighbor’s door and bluntly announce, “Hey, have you noticed I’m so much richer than you?” That’s not how these conversations work. He makes small talk, and in the middle of that small talk, he slips it in. “How’s it going? By the way, can you believe it? I just bought another luxury car and it’s already giving me problems. It’s my third one this month. How’s your bicycle, by the way?” He didn’t have to explicitly say “I am wealthier than you” - he said it between the lines. This is the nature of condescending conversation.

وَأَعَزُّ نَفَرًا - “And mightier in manpower” or “more respected in terms of supporters.” The word نَفَرًا refers to people who follow you, your employees, your workers, your supporters. An employer who has many workers enjoys two things: authority over them and respect from them. This man is saying, “I have more authority and more people showing me respect because of all the people who work for me.” He might say something like, “Oh, what village are you from? Yeah, I have three guys from your village working for me. Your cousin works for me actually - he shines my shoes. He’s pretty good at it. Must run in the family.” Slipping in these comments that establish superiority.

These conversations happen - not just between men, but perhaps even more commonly between women. One might say at a gathering, “Our daughter’s wedding was so expensive,” and go into elaborate detail about the venue and the arrangements, all while speaking to someone who had their daughter’s nikah at the masjid. What is the point of such conversation? It’s condescending talk from someone who sees themselves as above, directed at someone they view as below.

Ayah 35

وَدَخَلَ جَنَّتَهُۥ وَهُوَ ظَالِمٌ لِّنَفْسِهِۦ قَالَ مَآ أَظُنُّ أَن تَبِيدَ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أَبَدًا

After this condescending conversation with his neighbor, the man walks back. وَدَخَلَ جَنَّتَهُۥ - “And he entered his garden.” Notice it says جَنَّتَهُۥ - his garden, singular - not both his gardens. This makes sense because you can only physically enter one garden at a time. Some might wonder why it’s singular when he has two gardens, but he can’t exactly stand with one foot in one garden and the other foot in the other garden when there’s a river running between them.

وَهُوَ ظَالِمٌ لِّنَفْسِهِۦ - “While he was doing wrong to himself.” He had just wronged his neighbor with his arrogant words, but Allah ﷻ describes it as him wronging himself. This is profound - when we oppress others or display arrogance, we’re actually harming ourselves most of all.

He stands there in his garden, looking around. He sees the river flowing, the palm trees swaying, the fruits hanging perfectly, year after year of 100% productivity. He takes it all in and says: قَالَ مَآ أَظُنُّ أَن تَبِيدَ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أَبَدًا - “I do not think this will ever perish.”

تَبِيدَ refers to land dying - when the earth becomes barren, when plants dry up, when a garden meets its destruction. He’s convinced: “How could this ever go away? This is perfect. I’ve got the ideal setup. There’s a permanent water source that’s been running for generations. The weather is perfect. The farming is excellent. What more could I ask for? This is never going to end.”

Ayah 36

وَمَآ أَظُنُّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ قَآئِمَةً وَلَئِن رُّدِدتُّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّى لَأَجِدَنَّ خَيْرًا مِّنْهَا مُنقَلَبًا

This is where materialism leads. When someone becomes completely absorbed in this world, it necessarily pulls their focus away from the akhirah. This man was so lost in dunya that he reached the point where he said: وَمَآ أَظُنُّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ قَآئِمَةً - “And I do not think the Hour is coming.”

It’s not that he has some intellectual objection to the concept of resurrection. His problem isn’t philosophical - it’s spiritual. Materialism has made its way into his heart. His dunya has blinded him to the reality of the akhirah.

But then he adds something even more revealing: وَلَئِن رُّدِدتُّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّى - “And even if I was returned to my Lord.” Notice the phrasing. He doesn’t say “when I return” or even “if I return” with any sense of agency. He says رُّدِدتُّ - “if I was taken back,” “if I was returned” - passive voice, as though he’s being forced, as though it’s something happening to him against his preference. He doesn’t want to go back to his Master.

Then comes his stunning conclusion: لَأَجِدَنَّ خَيْرًا مِّنْهَا مُنقَلَبًا - “I will absolutely find better than this in return.” The word مُنقَلَبًا means in compensation, in return, as payment. His logic is: “Look at how much Allah ﷻ has given me here. Look at these two gardens, this productivity, this wealth. Obviously, this is from Allah ﷻ. I’m not a kafir - I acknowledge this is from Him. This proves that Allah ﷻ loves me. And if He loves me this much in this world and has taken such good care of me here, then obviously, if there is an afterlife, He’ll give me something even better there. In fact, if He takes me away from my awesome gardens, He’ll have to compensate me for my trouble with something superior.”

This is his mentality. Does this mentality exist today? Absolutely. Someone has built a mansion from money earned through haram means - through interest, through dishonest business, through illegal dealings. But they have Quranic calligraphy on their walls. They host gatherings where the Quran is recited. They say, “Alhamdulillah, Allah ﷻ has really blessed us. He’s been so good to us. Look at what He’s given us.”

But having wealth doesn’t mean you’re blessed - not if you’re earning it through haram. Don’t be confused. Don’t be deluded. That’s not a blessing in the sight of Allah ﷻ. This is exactly the delusion of the man with two gardens.

Ayah 37

قَالَ لَهُۥ صَاحِبُهُۥ وَهُوَ يُحَاوِرُهُۥٓ أَكَفَرْتَ بِٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكَ مِن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ سَوَّىٰكَ رَجُلًا

قَالَ لَهُۥ صَاحِبُهُۥ وَهُوَ يُحَاوِرُهُۥٓ - “His companion said to him while conversing with him.” The believing neighbor, who has been silent up until now, finally responds. Notice again the word يُحَاوِرُهُۥٓ - they’re in dialogue, in conversation. But the tone is about to shift dramatically.

أَكَفَرْتَ بِٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكَ - “Have you disbelieved in the One who created you?” This is a direct confrontation. The believing man doesn’t let his neighbor’s arrogance and delusion go unchallenged. He cuts straight to the heart of the matter: what his wealthy neighbor has been saying amounts to kufr - disbelief.

Then he reminds him of his origins: مِن تُرَابٍ - “from dust,” ثُمَّ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ - “then from a drop of fluid,” ثُمَّ سَوَّىٰكَ رَجُلًا - “then fashioned you into a man.”

This is the reality check. You’re standing there looking at your gardens, convinced of your permanence and superiority, but remember where you came from. You began as dust, then as a microscopic drop, and then Allah ﷻ shaped you and fashioned you into a complete human being. The One who created you from nothing, who can take it all away, who controls everything you think you own - have you really disbelieved in Him?

This is where the believing companion begins his response, reminding his arrogant neighbor of the fundamental truth he’s forgotten: we are all created beings, utterly dependent on our Creator, no matter how much wealth we accumulate in this temporary world.

Ayah 38–45 · linked video: 08. Al-Kahf (Ayah 38-45)

Surah Al-Kahf: Commentary on Ayahs 38-45

Ayah 38

لَّـٰكِنَّا۠ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ رَبِّى وَلَآ أُشْرِكُ بِرَبِّىٓ أَحَدًا

“However, as for me, He is Allah, my Master, and I do not associate anyone with my Master.”

The believer’s response begins with a profound declaration of tawheed. Notice how he starts with لَّـٰكِنَّا۠, which means “however” - but the word is incomplete, as if his humility prevents him from even fully pronouncing أَنَا (ana, meaning “I”). His ego is so small and his sense of servitude so complete that he can barely bring himself to refer to himself.

He declares: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ رَبِّى - “He is Allah ﷻ, my Master.” This is a direct counter to his friend’s arrogance. While the wealthy man took credit for everything, the believer acknowledges that Allah ﷻ is his true Master and Provider.

Then comes the crucial statement: وَلَآ أُشْرِكُ بِرَبِّىٓ أَحَدًا - “And I do not associate anyone with my Master at all.” This raises an important question: the wealthy gardener didn’t worship idols. He even mentioned Allah ﷻ when he said he might be returned to his Master. So what shirk is the believer referring to?

This is the shirk of misplaced trust and reliance. The wealthy man’s true reliance was on his garden, his investment, his workforce, his business plan. This is shirk of tawakkul - placing your ultimate trust in the means rather than in Allah ﷻ who controls all means.

We face this challenge constantly. When we turn the car ignition and the engine starts, we think it’s because we have a reliable car. When a headache goes away after taking medicine, we credit the medication. When a business succeeds, we point to our planning, our strategy, our hard work. But the believer maintains a different perspective: everything happens because Allah ﷻ wills it and allows it to happen.

The means are important - we must make effort, take medicine, work hard - but the actual results come only from Allah ﷻ. The wealthy man was taking credit for everything that made his business successful: his labor force, water supply, real estate pricing, satisfied customers. He saw all of these as the source of his success, forgetting the One who gave him all these means in the first place.

The believer is saying: I cannot afford to do that kind of shirk. I know I cannot afford many things in this worldly life, but one thing I absolutely cannot afford is shirk with Allah ﷻ. My reliance is on Him alone.

Ayah 39

وَلَوْلَآ إِذْ دَخَلْتَ جَنَّتَكَ قُلْتَ مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِٱللَّهِ ۚ إِن تَرَنِ أَنَا۠ أَقَلَّ مِنكَ مَالًا وَوَلَدًا

“And why did you not, when you entered your garden, say: ‘What Allah willed! There is no power except with Allah.’ If you see me as less than you in wealth and children…”

The believer continues his gentle advice with a profound question: وَلَوْلَآ إِذْ دَخَلْتَ جَنَّتَكَ - “Why wasn’t it the case that as you walked into your garden…” Picture them walking through the estate together, having this conversation among the palm trees and grapevines.

قُلْتَ مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ - “You said: ‘What Allah willed!’” We’ve now learned three essential phrases in this surah: Alhamdulillah (all praise belongs to Allah ﷻ), insha’Allah (if Allah ﷻ wills), and here, mashaa’Allah (what Allah ﷻ has willed).

Understanding mashaa’Allah is crucial. We often reserve this phrase only for happy occasions - someone got married, got a new job, had a baby. But mashaa’Allah applies to everything, good or bad by our perception, because it means: whatever is around me is the product of Allah’s will. Someone got sick? That too is mashaa’Allah - whatever Allah ﷻ willed has happened.

لَا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِٱللَّهِ - “There is no power except with Allah ﷻ.” This statement goes even deeper. There is no power in existence whatsoever, no ability to make anything happen, except through Allah ﷻ. Not just your garden’s success - everything.

When he says “you should have said mashaa’Allah,” he’s not just talking about the garden. He’s saying: whatever Allah ﷻ willed for me, and whatever He willed for you - these are His decisions. What He decided to give me and what He decided to give you is not about what we deserve or don’t deserve. Both wealth and poverty are tests from Allah ﷻ. Health is a test, sickness is a test. Having sons is a test, having daughters is a test. Being a parent is a test, being a child is a test. Everything is a test. Mashaa’Allah - that’s all there is to it.

إِن تَرَنِ أَنَا۠ أَقَلَّ مِنكَ مَالًا وَوَلَدًا - “If you really see me as less than you in terms of wealth and children…” The mention of children here carries economic weight. In that society, sons particularly were valuable as labor - they would work the land, continue the family business, and eventually care for their aging parents. Daughters, purely from a financial perspective in that context, were seen as an expense since they would marry into another household. The wealthy man had both material wealth and sons to help maintain it - he had everything going for him by worldly standards.

Ayah 40

فَعَسَىٰ رَبِّىٓ أَن يُؤْتِيَنِ خَيْرًا مِّن جَنَّتِكَ وَيُرْسِلَ عَلَيْهَا حُسْبَانًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَتُصْبِحَ صَعِيدًا زَلَقًا

“But perhaps my Master will give me better than your garden, and He will send upon it a precise strike from the sky, and it will become a slippery barren ground.”

The believer’s response is filled with hope and warning. فَعَسَىٰ رَبِّىٓ أَن يُؤْتِيَنِ خَيْرًا مِّن جَنَّتِكَ - “Perhaps my Master can give me a garden much better than yours.” He has complete conviction in this possibility because he knows he didn’t earn what he has, and Allah ﷻ is the one who distributes provision as He wills.

Then comes the warning: وَيُرْسِلَ عَلَيْهَا حُسْبَانًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ - “And He could send upon it husban from the sky.” The word حُسْبَان refers to a precisely timed destruction - some scholars interpret it as a lightning strike from the sky that creates a wildfire.

Imagine: one lightning bolt hits a palm tree. That tree catches fire. The fire spreads to the neighboring trees. As the trees burn, their foundations weaken and they begin to fall. When they fall, they crush the very delicate grape vines they were planted to protect. The security system becomes the reason for the garden’s destruction.

فَتُصْبِحَ صَعِيدًا زَلَقًا - “And it will become sa’id zalaq.” This describes land so completely dried up and barren that it just gathers dust. زَلَقًا means slippery - the ground becomes so flat and dried that when you walk on it, you slip and fall because there’s no softness. Fertile farmland is soft; your feet sink into it slightly. But when land is completely dry and dead, it becomes like a skating rink - smooth, hard, and slippery.

Ayah 41

أَوْ يُصْبِحَ مَآؤُهَا غَوْرًا فَلَن تَسْتَطِيعَ لَهُۥ طَلَبًا

“Or its water will sink into the earth, and you will never be able to seek it out.”

The believer presents another possibility: أَوْ يُصْبِحَ مَآؤُهَا غَوْرًا - “Or its water will become ghawr.” The word غَوْر means something that sinks deep into the earth, becoming unreachable. That river he was so proud of, that guaranteed water supply - it could simply be sucked into the earth.

فَلَن تَسْتَطِيعَ لَهُۥ طَلَبًا - “And you will never be able to seek it out.” Even if you dig and dig, searching desperately for that water, you won’t find it. The source of life for the entire estate, gone without a trace.

These aren’t just theoretical warnings. The believer is reminding his friend that everything he takes for granted - the trees, the fruit, the water - exists only by Allah’s permission and can be taken away in an instant.

Ayah 42

وَأُحِيطَ بِثَمَرِهِۦ فَأَصْبَحَ يُقَلِّبُ كَفَّيْهِ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَنفَقَ فِيهَا وَهِىَ خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا وَيَقُولُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى لَمْ أُشْرِكْ بِرَبِّىٓ أَحَدًا

“And his fruit was encompassed, and he began wringing his hands over what he had spent on it, while it had collapsed upon its trellises, and he said: ‘Oh, I wish I had not associated anything with my Master!’”

The warning became reality. وَأُحِيطَ بِثَمَرِهِۦ - “His fruit was surrounded.” Notice Allah ﷻ doesn’t say his gardens were surrounded or destroyed - He says his fruit was surrounded. Why? Because what he truly loved, what his heart was attached to, was the fruit. The thing he loved most was taken over, encompassed, destroyed.

فَأَصْبَحَ يُقَلِّبُ كَفَّيْهِ - “And he began turning his palms over.” The word كَفّ means the palm of your hand. This is what people do when they’re devastated, stressed beyond measure - they wring their hands, flip them over repeatedly, not knowing what to do with themselves.

عَلَىٰ مَآ أَنفَقَ فِيهَا - “Over everything he had spent on it.” All that money, all that investment, all those years of building and planning - gone.

وَهِىَ خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَ - “And it had collapsed upon its trellises.” The word عُرُوش refers to the delicate sticks and structures stuck in the ground that grape vines wrap around as they grow. The entire garden had tipped over, fallen on top of those fragile plants. The palm trees fell and crushed everything beneath them.

وَيَقُولُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى لَمْ أُشْرِكْ بِرَبِّىٓ أَحَدًا - “And he said: ‘Oh, if only I had not done shirk with my Master!’” This is the most important part. He makes tawbah. He recognizes his mistake. “If only I had listened to my friend!”

Here’s the beautiful ending: losing his wealth was actually the best thing that happened to him. Why? Because it saved him from spiritual destruction. His material loss led to his spiritual awakening. He realized the truth before it was too late. This is Allah’s mercy - sometimes what we perceive as disaster is actually divine intervention saving us from something far worse.

Ayah 43

وَلَمْ تَكُن لَّهُۥ فِئَةٌ يَنصُرُونَهُۥ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا كَانَ مُنتَصِرًا

“And there was no group to help him other than Allah, nor could he help himself.”

وَلَمْ تَكُن لَّهُۥ فِئَةٌ يَنصُرُونَهُۥ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ - “And he had no group that could help him besides Allah ﷻ.” The word فِئَة means a group that’s supposed to help you, support you, aid you. All those workers he employed, all those business connections, all those people who benefited from his success - where were they now? None of them could restore what Allah ﷻ had taken away.

وَمَا كَانَ مُنتَصِرًا - “And he could not even help himself.” He couldn’t take revenge on the situation, couldn’t fix it, couldn’t undo it. He was completely powerless.

This ayah teaches us about the reality of help and aid. Real support, real protection, real aid (نُصْرَة) comes only from Allah ﷻ. Everything else is temporary, conditional, and ultimately insufficient.

Ayah 44

هُنَالِكَ ٱلْوَلَـٰيَةُ لِلَّهِ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ هُوَ خَيْرٌ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ عُقْبًا

“In such situations, all protective aid belongs to Allah, the Truth. He is best in reward and best in outcome.”

هُنَالِكَ ٱلْوَلَـٰيَةُ لِلَّهِ ٱلْحَقِّ - “In those kinds of situations, the protective aid belongs only to the True Allah ﷻ.” The word وَلَايَة encompasses aid, help, protection, and taking responsibility. It’s not just assistance - it’s complete guardianship and support. And it belongs exclusively to Allah ﷻ, who is الْحَقّ - the Truth, the Real, the Ultimate Reality.

هُوَ خَيْرٌ ثَوَابًا - “He is far better in giving compensation.” Whatever you lose in this world, whatever Allah ﷻ takes away, His compensation is infinitely better. The reward He gives cannot be compared to anything worldly.

وَخَيْرٌ عُقْبًا - “And He is far better in terms of the final outcome.” The word عُقْب refers to the long-term result, the ultimate end. This garden’s destruction wasn’t the end of the story. The real end, the permanent outcome, the eternal result - that’s what matters. And Allah ﷻ is the best in determining those outcomes.

This is a reminder that what we see as endings are often not endings at all. The true conclusion of every matter is in the Hereafter, and that’s where Allah’s superior judgment will be fully manifest.

Ayah 45

وَٱضْرِبْ لَهُم مَّثَلَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا كَمَآءٍ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَٱخْتَلَطَ بِهِۦ نَبَاتُ ٱلْأَرْضِ فَأَصْبَحَ هَشِيمًا تَذْرُوهُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحُ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّقْتَدِرًا

“And present to them the example of the worldly life: it is like water which We send down from the sky, and the vegetation of the earth mingles with it, then it becomes debris scattered by the winds. And Allah is ever Perfect in Power over all things.”

Allah ﷻ now commands His Messenger ﷺ to give an example that crystallizes everything we’ve learned: وَٱضْرِبْ لَهُم مَّثَلَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا - “Give them the example of worldly life.”

كَمَآءٍ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ - “It is like water that comes down from the sky.” Just as rain falls from the heavens by Allah’s command.

فَٱخْتَلَطَ بِهِۦ نَبَاتُ ٱلْأَرْضِ - “Then the plants and nutrients of the earth start mixing with it.” The word اخْتَلَطَ means two things joining together, mixing, becoming intertwined. The water combines with the nutrients in the soil, and vegetation begins to grow.

فَأَصْبَحَ هَشِيمًا - “Then it became hashim.” The word هَشِيم means shredded, broken into pieces, defeated, crushed, torn up. The crops that once grew green and beautiful eventually dry up, die, and disintegrate into worthless debris.

تَذْرُوهُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحُ - “The winds scatter it.” The word ذَرَى means to throw something up into the air. These crops became so light, so crusty, so worthless that even the winds can carry away what’s left of them.

This is the nature of worldly life. Things come, they grow, they reach their peak, and then they fade away. They come and they go, come and go, in endless cycles. Whatever success we enjoy in this world, whatever achievement, whatever accumulation - it’s all temporary.

Notice what started the farm’s growth: water from the sky, a blessing from Allah ﷻ. But even that blessed growth eventually reached death and decay. So whatever goodness we experience in this life, whatever success we taste, comes from Allah ﷻ - but it won’t be permanent. Just as He sends rain to raise crops that eventually turn to dust, He grants us blessings in this world that are inherently temporary.

And just like that farm, you and I will one day also be reduced to dust. This is the reality we must internalize.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّقْتَدِرًا - “And Allah has always been in absolute, complete control over all things.” The verb كَانَ in the past tense indicates this has always been true and always will be. The word مُقْتَدِر means having complete power, perfect ability, total control with precise calculation over everything.

You have no control. He has always been and will always be in complete control over all these events - the rising and falling, the growth and decay, the giving and taking away.

This parable settles in our hearts what our attitude toward this worldly life should be. The primary focus of these ayahs is attacking materialism - the obsession with worldly gain, the belief that accumulation equals success, the trust placed in wealth and status instead of in Allah ﷻ. This is the great fitnah of our time, and these ayahs dismantle it completely, reminding us that everything in this world is as temporary as crops that grow and wither, and that real success lies in recognizing Allah’s absolute control and placing our trust in Him alone.

Ayah 46–57 · linked video: 09. Al-Kahf (Ayah 46-57)

Ayah 46

ٱلْمَالُ وَٱلْبَنُونَ زِينَةُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَٱلْبَـٰقِيَـٰتُ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتُ خَيْرٌ عِندَ رَبِّكَ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ أَمَلًا

Allah ﷻ provides a profound summary of what captures human attention in this worldly life. المال والبنون - wealth and sons - are described as the زينة, the beauty and adornment of this temporary existence. It’s important to understand that بنون carries a broader meaning than simply biological children. This term encompasses manpower, employees, armies, your crew, your team - essentially anyone who works under you or follows your leadership.

But then Allah ﷻ redirects our focus to what truly matters: والباقيات الصالحات - the remaining good deeds. The very structure of this phrase is instructive. الباقيات means “the remaining things” or “the lasting things.” These are the deeds that endure beyond your life in this world. Allah ﷻ tells us these lasting righteous deeds are خير عند ربك ثوابا - better with your Master in terms of reward and compensation.

People invest their hopes in their wealth, planning for retirement, for their children’s future, for financial security. They invest hopes in their children, imagining what they’ll become, how they’ll carry on the family name. But Allah ﷻ says وخير أملا - these lasting good deeds are better as a source of hope. Why? Because they’re connected to the real future, the Akhirah. The deeds you do that benefit others, the legacy of goodness you leave behind - these are what you should place your hopes in, because the true future isn’t measured in years but in eternity.

Ayah 47

وَيَوْمَ نُسَيِّرُ ٱلْجِبَالَ وَتَرَى ٱلْأَرْضَ بَارِزَةً وَحَشَرْنَـٰهُمْ فَلَمْ نُغَادِرْ مِنْهُمْ أَحَدًا

Having established what truly matters in this life, Allah ﷻ now paints a vivid picture of why we shouldn’t place excessive hope in this temporary world. يوم نسير الجبال - on the day We will make the mountains move. The word سير means to walk casually, effortlessly. Mountains - these massive, immovable objects that define landscapes - will be moving around as if they’re nothing, as though they’re strolling leisurely.

وترى الأرض بارزة - and you will see the earth laid bare. The word بارز comes from برز, which means to come face to face with something, to emerge from concealment. We see the earth every day, but we don’t see what it’s hiding. On that Day, the earth will reveal all its secrets, everything it has been concealing all along.

Picture this scene: mountains are shaking and moving effortlessly, the earth is bringing out everything it had hidden, and amid this chaos, وحشرناهم - We will gather them. The word حشر is particularly significant here because it’s used for herding animals, not the usual word for gathering people, which is جمع. This choice of word emphasizes the state of panic and chaos - people will be running like wild animals, all being herded in one direction.

فلم نغادر منهم أحدا - We will not leave behind a single one of them. The verb غادر in classical Arabic means to spare someone, to leave someone behind. You might select an army and leave the weak behind, or punish a group but spare someone who looks particularly remorseful. But Allah ﷻ makes it absolutely clear: not a single soul will be left behind or spared from this gathering.

Ayah 48

وَعُرِضُوا۟ عَلَىٰ رَبِّكَ صَفًّا لَّقَدْ جِئْتُمُونَا كَمَا خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍۭ ۚ بَلْ زَعَمْتُمْ أَلَّن نَّجْعَلَ لَكُم مَّوْعِدًا

وعرضوا على ربك صفا - they will be presented before your Master in rows upon rows. The chaos ends, and now comes the order, the arrangement, the presentation before Allah ﷻ. This recalls the initial creation mentioned in Surah Al-A’raf, where we stood before Allah ﷻ the first time.

Then the divine address begins: لقد جئتمونا كما خلقناكم أول مرة - You have indeed come to Us just as We created you the first time. This is the second meeting, but بل زعمتم - rather, you were in confident assumption. You were so sure ألن نجعل لكم موعدا - that We would never set an appointed time with you. The word موعد means an appointment, a promised meeting time. They lived their entire lives convinced this moment would never come, and now here it is.

Ayah 49

وَوُضِعَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ فَتَرَى ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ مُشْفِقِينَ مِمَّا فِيهِ وَيَقُولُونَ يَـٰوَيْلَتَنَا مَالِ هَـٰذَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا يُغَادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَلَا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّآ أَحْصَىٰهَا ۚ وَوَجَدُوا۟ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ حَاضِرًا ۗ وَلَا يَظْلِمُ رَبُّكَ أَحَدًا

ووضع الكتاب - and the book will be placed down, dropped before them. Then comes one of the most powerful visualizations in the Quran: فترى المجرمين مشفقين - you will see the criminals in a state of إشفاق.

To understand إشفاق, we need to appreciate the imagery of Arabic. The word comes from شفق, which refers to that part of the day when light and darkness coexist - when the sun turns orange, clouds cover it, and day is giving way to darkness but hasn’t fully surrendered yet. It’s simultaneously light and dark. This is why إشفاق in Arabic combines two emotions: love and fear. You love to be saved, but you’re overwhelmed by fear. And just as darkness eventually wins over the fading light of شفق, so too does fear dominate over hope in the word إشفاق.

So these criminals see their book, and perhaps there’s some element of hope - maybe there’s a good deed recorded somewhere - but overwhelmingly, مما فيه - they are terrified of what’s in it. Fear is the dominant emotion washing over them.

ويقولون يا ويلتنا - and they cry out, “Woe to us!” or as old English might render it, “What terrible destruction has befallen us!” Then comes their observation about the book: مال هذا الكتاب - What is wrong with this book? لا يغادر صغيرة ولا كبيرة - It doesn’t spare the smallest detail nor the biggest event. It made no distinction between the minor and the major.

إلا أحصاها - except that it counted them, enumerated them like pebbles are counted. Every single deed, numbered and recorded.

Notice the powerful shift in tenses here: ووجدوا ما عملوا حاضرا - and they found what they had done standing right in front of them. If Allah ﷻ had said يجدون in the present tense, it would suggest they keep finding more and more, a gradual discovery. But the past tense وجدوا indicates one overwhelming moment - all their deeds piled into one colossal monument, presented to them in one instantaneous showing, standing face to face with them.

ولا يظلم ربك أحدا - and your Master will not wrong anyone. Not a single soul will face injustice in this accounting.

Ayah 50

وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْجِنِّ فَفَسَقَ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِۦٓ ۗ أَفَتَتَّخِذُونَهُۥ وَذُرِّيَّتَهُۥٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِى وَهُمْ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّۢ ۚ بِئْسَ لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بَدَلًا

Now we reach a critical transition. The human story that began with judgment now goes back to its beginning: وإذ قلنا للملائكة اسجدوا لآدم - when We said to the angels, “Prostrate to Adam.” فسجدوا إلا إبليس - so they prostrated, except Iblis.

Here comes the crucial clarification: كان من الجن (kana min al-jinn) - he was from the jinn. This is directly addressing a false belief in Christian theology about Iblis being a “fallen angel.” Allah ﷻ makes it explicit: No, he was from the jinn. ففسق عن أمر ربه - therefore, as a result of being from the jinn, he violated the command of his Master.

The word فسق means to violate, to transgress. Angels cannot violate Allah’s commands - it’s not in their nature. But jinn can, and Iblis did. The ف here indicates causation: because he was a jinn, therefore this violation was possible for him. Notice that Allah ﷻ emphasizes action - فسق is active disobedience, a deliberate violation of command.

This emphasis on deeds throughout the surah is particularly significant for addressing the Christian community, who had come to believe that deeds are secondary, that faith alone saves you regardless of your actions. Allah ﷻ is establishing that the very problem with Iblis was his violation of God’s command.

أفتتخذونه وذريته أولياء من دوني - Will you then take him and his offspring as protective friends and guardians instead of Me? وهم لكم عدو - when they are clearly enemies to you?

This connects powerfully to an earlier conversation in the surah. The disbelievers had said, “Why don’t You just send an angel?” They wanted to follow an angel, not a human messenger. Allah ﷻ responds here: You claim you want to follow angels because they’re perfect, yet all you’ve been following is Shaytan. Human beings are in the middle - why do you always have to pick extremes? You reject the human messenger and instead follow the worst of creation.

بئس للظالمين بدلا - what a terrible alternative for the wrongdoers! What a terrible exchange they’ve made.

Ayah 51

۞ مَّآ أَشْهَدتُّهُمْ خَلْقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا خَلْقَ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَمَا كُنتُ مُتَّخِذَ ٱلْمُضِلِّينَ عَضُدًا

ما أشهدتهم خلق السماوات والأرض - I did not make them witnesses to the creation of the heavens and the earth. Allah ﷻ is speaking about these false gods that people worship, these beings they’ve elevated to divine status. They weren’t present when He created the universe.

ولا خلق أنفسهم - nor were they witnesses to their own creation. They weren’t even present when Allah ﷻ created them. How can beings who were absent from their own creation be worthy of worship?

وما كنت متخذ المضلين عضدا - and I would never take those who misguide as عضد. The word عضد refers to the muscle from the elbow to the shoulder - the part you show off when you flex your muscles. In Arabic, this expression means backup, support, muscle. When something’s about to go down and you need backup, you call for عضد.

Allah ﷻ is declaring: I don’t need backup. I don’t need support. And why would I ever need backup from these misguided creatures you worship? The entire premise is absurd.

Ayah 52

وَيَوْمَ يَقُولُ نَادُوا۟ شُرَكَآءِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ فَدَعَوْهُمْ فَلَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَهُمْ وَجَعَلْنَا بَيْنَهُم مَّوْبِقًا

ويوم يقول نادوا شركائي الذين زعمتم - and the Day when He will say, “Call upon My ‘partners’ whom you claimed.” Go ahead - call Zeus, call Isa (AS), call the Holy Spirit, call the mother of God, call all of them. Call these partners you were so confident in, about whom you placed all your assumptions and theological theories.

فدعوهم - so they will call them. فلم يستجيبوا لهم - but they will not respond to them. The verb استجاب is crucial here. It’s not just جاب meaning “to respond.” The است prefix indicates effort, attempt. Allah ﷻ didn’t say فلم يجيبوا لهم - “they won’t respond” - which would leave open the possibility that they tried but failed. No, He said فلم يستجيبوا - they won’t even try to respond. They won’t even attempt it. They won’t even care to respond.

وجعلنا بينهم موبقا - and We will place between them a مَوْبِق. This word comes from وبق, meaning destruction or death. A مَوْبِق is a place of destruction, a valley of death. Picture the people of the right hand separated from the people of the left, with a massive chasm between them. The worshippers will see the prophets and beings they worshipped, they’ll start calling out to them desperately, but Allah ﷻ will create this insurmountable gap between them.

Ayah 53

وَرَءَا ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ ٱلنَّارَ فَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُم مُّوَاقِعُوهَا وَلَمْ يَجِدُوا۟ عَنْهَا مَصْرِفًا

ورأى المجرمون النار - and the criminals will see the Fire. فظنوا أنهم مواقعوها - and they will realize they are about to fall into it. The word وقع means to fall hard, to thump down. مواقعوها uses the most intense form - it’s not just falling, but the struggle of trying not to fall while falling violently anyway.

Imagine being thrown down a cliff, trying desperately to grab onto something, anything, but continuing to fall. You keep grasping, keep struggling, but the fall is inevitable and violent. That’s the image captured in this word - they realize they’re going down, and it’s not going to be gentle.

ولم يجدوا عنها مصرفا - and they will find no escape from it. مَصْرِف is a place to turn away to, an exit route, an alternative path. But there will be none. Once they begin that fall, there’s no way out.

Ayah 54

وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنَا فِى هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ لِلنَّاسِ مِن كُلِّ مَثَلٍ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَكْثَرَ شَىْءٍ جَدَلًا

ولقد صرفنا في هذا القرآن للناس من كل مثل - and We have certainly explained in this Quran for mankind every kind of example in multiple ways. The word صَرَّفْنا means We have alternated, We have varied. It comes from صرف, which means to turn, to alternate, to change. That’s why we call grammar الصرف - because we change and alternate the forms of words.

Allah ﷻ is saying: We’ve given the same truths in different ways, from different angles, through different stories and parables. One way, then another way, then yet another way. We’ve alternated and varied the presentation so that every type of person, every type of mind, can find a way to understand.

وكان الإنسان أكثر شيء جدلا - yet the human being remains, in most things, extremely argumentative. The word جَدَل means aggressive argumentation, the kind where you’re not seeking truth but seeking to win, to refute, to tear down. The human being’s first impulse upon hearing something, even truth, is often negative and critical. “I don’t think so. I don’t like it. This doesn’t make sense to me.” The immediate reaction is to be defensive, to argue, to push back.

Ayah 55

وَمَا مَنَعَ ٱلنَّاسَ أَن يُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ إِذْ جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْهُدَىٰ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن تَأْتِيَهُمْ سُنَّةُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ أَوْ يَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ قُبُلًا

وما منع الناس أن يؤمنوا إذ جاءهم الهدى - and what prevented people from believing when guidance came to them, ويستغفروا ربهم - and from asking forgiveness of their Master?

إلا أن تأتيهم سنة الأولين - except that they await the same fate that befell those before them. The word سُنَّة here means tradition, pattern, the established way. They want to walk the same path, fall into the same traps, repeat the same mistakes over and over again. What prevented them? They insisted on repeating the errors of previous generations.

أو يأتيهم العذاب قُبُلا - or they wait until the punishment comes to them قُبُلا - face to face, right in front of them, undeniable and immediate. They won’t believe until it’s too late, until they’re staring at the consequences directly.

Ayah 56

وَمَا نُرْسِلُ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ ۚ وَيُجَـٰدِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ لِيُدْحِضُوا۟ بِهِ ٱلْحَقَّ ۖ وَٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ ءَايَـٰتِى وَمَآ أُنذِرُوا۟ هُزُوًا

وما نرسل المرسلين إلا مبشرين ومنذرين - and We do not send messengers except as bearers of good news and warners. That’s their purpose - to bring glad tidings to those who respond, and to warn those who turn away.

ويجادل الذين كفروا بالباطل - yet the disbelievers argue using falsehood. ليدحضوا به الحق - so they can dismantle the truth by means of it. The word يُدْحِضوا comes from دَحَض, which means to slip, to make something slide and crack, to undermine its foundation. When you present proof for something and someone tries to show that your proof is worthless, that it doesn’t withstand scrutiny - that’s دَحْض.

Allah ﷻ is describing their strategy: they use falsehood to try to poke holes in the truth, to dismantle the arguments of faith, to make the solid foundations of religion slip and crack. “How does this make sense? This isn’t logical. That’s contradictory.” They’re not seeking understanding - they’re seeking to demolish.

واتخذوا آياتي وما أنذروا هزوا - and they took My miraculous signs and whatever warnings they were given as a joke, as mockery. This is perhaps the most powerful tool for undermining someone’s message. You don’t even need to present a counter-argument. You just need to scoff. Someone presents their case, and you simply laugh dismissively. “Really? Paradise? Sure.” That sneer, that scoff - it’s enough to make the speaker feel foolish without you having said a single word of rebuttal. It’s a devastatingly effective tool in the hands of those destined for the Fire.

Ayah 57

وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن ذُكِّرَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِۦ فَأَعْرَضَ عَنْهَا وَنَسِىَ مَا قَدَّمَتْ يَدَاهُ ۚ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً أَن يَفْقَهُوهُ وَفِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَقْرًا ۖ وَإِن تَدْعُهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ فَلَن يَهْتَدُوٓا۟ إِذًا أَبَدًا

This is one of the most sobering ayahs in the Quran, and it should strike fear into every heart that engages with Allah’s revelation.

ومن أظلم ممن ذكر بآيات ربه فأعرض عنها - and who could be a worse wrongdoer than someone who was reminded of the ayat of his Master, then ignored them? Who could be worse than someone who heard the Quran, who was given advice and counsel through Allah’s words, then turned away from them?

Now, this is directed at the kuffar, at those who reject faith entirely. But we need to pause here. Every Friday, we hear ayat of the Quran in salah and khutbah. These ayat give advice about our lives, guidance for our choices, warnings about our behavior. Are we listening? Or are we ignoring them after the fact? This warning isn’t limited to the disbelievers.

ونسي ما قدمت يداه - and he forgot what his own hands had sent forward. First, he ignores the reminder. The opposite of ذِكْر (remembrance) is نِسْيان (forgetfulness). So he forgets the message, and worse, he forgets his own deeds, his own actions, his own mistakes.

Think about what Allah ﷻ is teaching us here: when we’re being reminded through the Quran, what helps us truly take that reminder to heart? It’s keeping track of our own deeds, our behaviors, our past mistakes - the things we’re going to have to answer Allah ﷻ for. When you think about those things, when you honestly assess your own actions, it becomes easier to pay attention to Allah’s book. But when you’re oblivious to your past, when you think, “Oh, it’s all covered, it’s all forgiven, no need to worry about what I’ve done” - that’s when you stop caring about what the Quran has to say. You’ll only engage with it superficially.

إنا جعلنا على قلوبهم أكنة أن يفقهوه - indeed, We have placed upon their hearts coverings that prevent them from understanding it. We’ve seen the word أَكِنَّة before - it’s a seal, a lock, a covering that prevents light from entering. And we know that revelation is light. So for these people, their hearts are sealed off from the light of understanding.

وفي آذانهم وقرا - and in their ears is a barrier, a filter. Even if they hear, they don’t really hear.

But here we must understand something critical: there’s a difference between understanding and internalizing. The word فِقْه doesn’t just mean understanding - it means deep understanding that has penetrated you, that has become part of you, that you’ve internalized. You might understand that truth is good, but have you internalized it? You might understand that anger is harmful, but have you deeply absorbed that knowledge so it changes your behavior?

These people might have understanding - they might be able to quote ayat, explain grammar, discuss vocabulary, present scholarly research - but they haven’t internalized any of it. It hasn’t gone deep. They have knowledge, but it hasn’t become part of who they are.

Let me give you an example from within the religious community. Someone gives a lecture or a khutbah. You’re listening, and your mind is thinking: “Oh, that was pretty good. If he had added this point or that point, it would have been better. His delivery here could have been smoother.” Then you write him an email afterwards: “Pretty good speech, but next time if you add these three points, it’ll be even better. Here’s a cool quote you could throw in.”

That person - do you think adding those points would help them appreciate the message more? No. They’re treating the entire act of listening as a performance review, as entertainment to be critiqued. They’re listening, but they’re not listening. Why do we listen to speeches about the deen? It’s not a form of entertainment. If that’s the state we’ve reached, it’s truly sad.

Or consider someone taking notes during a lecture - pages upon pages upon pages, an entire encyclopedia in their notebook. But all of this is so they can teach others later. It’s not for themselves. They’re not taking it into their own heart. My uncle used to say: ما خرج من القلب يصل إلى القلب - what comes from the heart reaches the heart. And what doesn’t come from the heart will never reach another heart. If you’re not taking knowledge into your heart, you’ll never give it to anyone else’s heart. It will just be an artificial transfer of information.

This is a truly frightening state to be in, and it’s what I fear most for myself and for all of us - that our relationship with revelation would become so shallow, so superficial, so disconnected from our hearts.

وإن تدعهم إلى الهدى فلن يهتدوا إذا أبدا - and if you call them to guidance, they will never be guided, ever. This is the final, devastating consequence. When hearts are sealed, when understanding is blocked, when people engage with revelation without internalizing it - guidance becomes impossible. Not just unlikely, but impossible. فلن يهتدوا - they will never be guided. أبدا - ever.

May Allah ﷻ protect us from becoming people who hear His words but don’t truly listen, who understand intellectually but never internalize, who engage with the Quran but never let it penetrate our hearts. May He make us among those who, when reminded by His ayat, take that reminder to heart and let it change us from within.

Ayah 58–73 · linked video: 10. Al-Kahf (Ayah 58-73)

Surah Al-Kahf: Commentary on Ayahs 58-73

Ayah 58

وَرَبُّكَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ذُو ٱلرَّحْمَةِ ۖ لَوْ يُؤَاخِذُهُم بِمَا كَسَبُوا۟ لَعَجَّلَ لَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابَ ۚ بَل لَّهُم مَّوْعِدٌ لَّن يَجِدُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦ مَوْئِلًا

Your Master is الْغَفُورُ, exceedingly forgiving. He is ذُو ٱلرَّحْمَةِ the One who possesses and owns mercy itself. The proof of His forgiveness and mercy is made clear in what follows: if He were to seize people immediately because of what they’ve earned, then punishment would have been expedited for them right away. But this is not how Allah ﷻ operates. Rather, the reality is that they have an appointed time, a deadline set for them.

The ayah concludes with a powerful image: لَّن يَجِدُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦ مَوْئِلًا (they will not find any refuge besides Him). The word مَوْئِل refers to a barn or fenced-off field where shepherds keep camels and sheep safe from wolves and thieves, protected by a good security system. The imagery suggests that when that appointed time comes, people will try to hide like frightened animals, but they won’t find any place of safety or escape.

Ayah 59

وَتِلْكَ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ لَمَّا ظَلَمُوا۟ وَجَعَلْنَا لِمَهْلِكِهِم مَّوْعِدًا

This ayah extends the warning beyond individuals to entire civilizations. Those towns, those complete societies—We destroyed them when they committed wrong. The word لَمَّا is linguistically significant here. Unlike إِذْ, which simply means “when,” lamma carries the meaning of continuation until a final point. It indicates that these nations reached a stage where the only thing that could be said about them was that they had become people of wrongdoing. It wasn’t just one deed of wrong, but rather their entire legacy amounted to nothing but oppression and injustice.

The word مَهْلِك combines both time and place of destruction. Allah ﷻ appointed both when and where their destruction would occur. Consider Fir’awn (Pharaoh), who lived in his castle in Egypt, seemingly secure in his power. Yet Allah ﷻ had already set a specific date and place for his end. He wouldn’t die in his fortress or on any battlefield. The appointed place was water—a humble death by drowning for one who claimed divinity. This is the meaning of مَوْعِد, the appointed time that cannot be escaped.

Ayah 60

وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِفَتَىٰهُ لَآ أَبْرَحُ حَتَىٰٓ أَبْلُغَ مَجْمَعَ ٱلْبَحْرَيْنِ أَوْ أَمْضِىَ حُقُبًا

Now we arrive at the third and second-to-last story in the surah. Between the previous stories, we had warnings and reminders. After the story of the people of the cave, there was a brief sermon. Between that story and the tale of the two gardens, another warning appeared. Now, before this third narrative begins, we again find cautionary verses. This structure shows how the surah weaves together storytelling and guidance.

Musa (AS) said to his فَتَى, his young companion and assistant. Some scholars, including Ibn Abbas (RA), identify this young man as Yusha ibn Nun (AS), who would later become a prophet himself. What a remarkable opportunity—to travel with and learn from a messenger of Allah ﷻ. This is the second time in the surah we encounter a fata, a young person in service to knowledge. Between the lines, we learn an important lesson about seeking sacred knowledge: if you cannot find a messenger, find those who carry the message of the messengers. Be in the company of scholars, serve them and learn from them.

Notice that this young man isn’t sitting in a formal study circle conducting intensive lessons. He’s rowing a boat, helping with the journey, doing whatever needs to be done. The point is to be in the company of knowledge, to serve and observe. When I was young, I sought the company of scholars in whatever capacity I could.

Musa (AS) declares his commitment: لَآ أَبْرَحُ (I will not stop, I will not cease). He is determined and fully committed to this mission. He will continue حَتَّىٰٓ أَبْلُغَ مَجْمَعَ ٱلْبَحْرَيْنِ (until I reach the meeting place of the two seas). This refers to two rivers or bodies of water that merge together. From what we understand, there may have been a rock or mountain between them, and as the land narrows, the two waters eventually meet.

Then Musa (AS) adds: أَوْ أَمْضِىَ حُقُبًا (or I will continue for huquba—eons upon eons). According to the most conservative linguistic estimate, a huqub represents an 80-year period, roughly a human lifetime. The plural huquba suggests multiple lifetimes. What Musa (AS) is saying is: “I don’t care if I have to spend numerous lifetimes searching. I will keep going until I find that place.”

The backstory we learn from hadith literature tells us that Allah ﷻ informed Musa (AS) that there was someone more knowledgeable than him, and that he should seek this person out at the place where two oceans meet. Musa (AS) demonstrates incredible dedication to learning. What makes this even more remarkable is that we already have a teacher-student relationship established between Musa (AS) and his young assistant. Yet here is the teacher himself seeking to become a student. A real teacher is someone who says, “I need a teacher.” This is the beautiful dynamic of knowledge in Islam—no matter how much you know, you remain a student, always seeking to learn more.

Ayah 61

فَلَمَّا بَلَغَا مَجْمَعَ بَيْنِهِمَا نَسِيَا حُوتَهُمَا فَٱتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُۥ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ سَرَبًا

When they both reached the junction between the two bodies of water, something unexpected happened. Notice the dual form نَسِيَا —they both forgot. They both forgot their حُوت, their fish.

Before we continue, let’s understand the context: Yusha (AS) had been given instructions to pack food for the journey, and they took a fish with them. This wasn’t a small goldfish—it was a large fish, the kind that could feed travelers. They had already roasted and cooked it because obviously, cooking a fish on a small boat during the journey would be impractical. So they prepared it beforehand.

Now imagine the scene: perhaps Musa (AS) is taking a rest, letting the young man navigate, telling him to alert him if anything significant happens. The young companion is alone with the task of watching and rowing. On a long journey with no scenery, nothing but water, and with his teacher asleep, he has nothing to distract him. In those days, there were no phones, no books to read while traveling, no entertainment. Just the monotony of the journey. He might start singing songs he knows, but eventually even that becomes tiresome. The line between wakefulness and drowsiness, between reality and imagination, begins to blur. You question whether you’re truly awake or half-dreaming—anyone who has been on an endless road trip understands this state.

In this state, something miraculous occurred. The fish, which was already cooked and dead, somehow came back to life. فَٱتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُۥ (It took its path)—notice the language suggests the fish’s course was pre-programmed, as though its biology was designed for this very moment. It took the path it was created to take فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ سَرَبًا (in the sea, making a tunnel).

The word سَرَب literally means a tunnel, and it also evokes the imagery of water in the desert—a mirage. The fish created a wake in the water, like when a speedboat moves quickly and leaves a trail behind it. If you looked at this wake from above, it would appear like an arrow pointing in a specific direction: “That way. That’s where you need to go.”

The young man saw this incredible sight—a cooked fish coming back to life, jumping into the water, making waves and a clear path. He thought it was amazing, but then, in his state between sleep and waking, he didn’t process its significance. Perhaps he thought he had imagined it. So he said nothing, and they kept going.

Ayah 62

فَلَمَّا جَاوَزَا قَالَ لِفَتَىٰهُ ءَاتِنَا غَدَآءَنَا لَقَدْ لَقِينَا مِن سَفَرِنَا هَـٰذَا نَصَبًا

فَلَمَّا جَاوَزَا—when they crossed beyond it. The word جَاوَزَ literally means to pass by or cross over. They were supposed to stop when the two bodies of water met. The only sign that occurred at that exact moment was the fish jumping out, which should have been their indicator to stop. But they didn’t stop—they kept going, passing beyond the appointed place.

After some time, Musa (AS) woke up and asked his companion how things were going. Everything seemed fine, but then hunger set in. He said, “Bring us our lunch.” Then he observed: لَقَدْ لَقِينَا مِن سَفَرِنَا هَـٰذَا نَصَبًا (We have certainly suffered from this journey of ours, exhaustion). The word نَصَب indicates deep fatigue and weariness.

Some commentators note something significant here: they wouldn’t have felt this hunger and exhaustion if they had stopped where they were supposed to stop. The fact that they felt such intense hunger was itself a sign that they had gone beyond their intended destination. The hunger was an alarm that they had missed their mark.

Ayah 63

قَالَ أَرَءَيْتَ إِذْ أَوَيْنَآ إِلَى ٱلصَّخْرَةِ فَإِنِّى نَسِيتُ ٱلْحُوتَ وَمَآ أَنسَىٰنِيهُ إِلَّا ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَنْ أَذْكُرَهُۥ ۚ وَٱتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُۥ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ عَجَبًا

Now it’s the young man’s turn to speak. He turns around to get the fish, and suddenly the memory floods back. He turns back to Musa (AS) and says, أَرَءَيْتَ (Did you see)—but obviously Musa (AS) didn’t see anything; he was asleep.

إِذْ أَوَيْنَآ إِلَى ٱلصَّخْرَةِ (When we rested by the rock)—فَإِنِّى نَسِيتُ ٱلْحُوتَ (I forgot the fish). Something incredible happened, and he’s still not entirely sure whether he dreamed it or if it actually occurred.

Then he immediately offers an excuse: وَمَآ أَنسَىٰنِيهُ إِلَّا ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ (Nothing made me forget it except Shaytan). He’s saying, “It wasn’t really my fault—Shaytan made me forget to tell you.” Some scholars derive from this that the only real power Shaytan has over us is to make us forget. He can’t force us to do evil, but he can make us forget what’s important, forget our priorities, forget to mention crucial information.

We can also observe something about human nature here, particularly young people: they’re very skilled at making excuses when they mess up. But let’s return to something mentioned earlier. In Ayah 61, when the fish jumped out, the Quran said نَسِيَا (they both forgot)—using the dual form. Both of them share responsibility. The first mention of forgetfulness applied to both the leader and his assistant. This teaches us something about leadership and responsibility: if a subordinate makes a mistake, the leader still bears responsibility. An amir must take responsibility for the errors of those under his authority. The frightening implication for teachers is that they are responsible for the failures of their students.

The young man concludes his explanation: وَٱتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُۥ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ عَجَبًا (And it took its course into the sea in an amazing way). He calls it عَجَب—something wondrous and strange. After all, a roasted fish coming back to life, jumping into the water and creating waves is extraordinary.

Ayah 64

قَالَ ذَٰلِكَ مَا كُنَّا نَبْغِ ۚ فَٱرْتَدَّا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَارِهِمَا قَصَصًا

Musa (AS) immediately understood the significance. He said, ذَٰلِكَ مَا كُنَّا نَبْغِ (That is what we were seeking). That strange occurrence with the fish—that was exactly the sign we needed.

فَٱرْتَدَّا (So they returned)—they turned back, retracing their path عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَارِهِمَا (upon their footsteps) قَصَصًا (precisely, following the tracks). The word قَصَص comes from the same root as قَصَّ, to trace or follow closely. It’s the same word used when the mother of Musa (AS) told her daughter فَقُصِّيهِ (follow him)—meaning trace his steps very carefully, don’t lose sight of him. Here too, they traced their steps back with great precision, looking for the exact spot where the miracle occurred.

Ayah 65

فَوَجَدَا عَبْدًا مِّنْ عِبَادِنَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا وَعَلَّمْنَـٰهُ مِن لَّدُنَّا عِلْمًا

فَوَجَدَا (Then they found) عَبْدًا مِّنْ عِبَادِنَآ (a slave from among Our slaves). Notice how Allah ﷻ keeps this person’s identity anonymous. The Quran doesn’t provide a name. Later Islamic literature refers to this figure as al-Khidr, and various opinions exist about his nature. Some say he was a very old man given thousands of years of life, someone who still walks the earth. Others, including some companions like Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA), held the opinion that he was an angel. When people started naming their children “Khidr,” Umar (RA) objected, suggesting they shouldn’t name children after angels. Other scholars firmly maintained he was human, albeit granted exceptional longevity.

But here’s what matters: all you need to know is عَبْدًا مِّنْ عِبَادِنَآ—a servant from among Our servants. If you start asking too many questions about his identity, you might miss the point of the story entirely. The lesson isn’t about who he was, but about what he represents regarding knowledge, wisdom and the limits of human understanding. Let it go and focus on what Allah ﷻ is teaching us through this narrative.

The ayah describes this servant with two qualities: ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا (We had given him mercy from Ourselves especially) وَعَلَّمْنَـٰهُ مِن لَّدُنَّا عِلْمًا (and We had taught him knowledge from Our presence).

Notice the subtle difference in prepositions. The first phrase uses مِنْ عِندِنَا, “from with Us,” while the second uses مِن لَّدُنَّا, “from directly from Our presence.” The second carries more emphasis, suggesting the knowledge given to him comes even more directly from Allah ﷻ than the mercy. Some scholars speculate about what this special mercy might be—perhaps an extended lifespan, or some other unique blessing. But what’s being highlighted most is the extraordinary knowledge he possessed, knowledge that came directly from Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 66

قَالَ لَهُۥ مُوسَىٰ هَلْ أَتَّبِعُكَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن تُعَلِّمَنِ مِمَّا عُلِّمْتَ رُشْدًا

Now observe the beautiful transition happening in this narrative. Up until this point, we had a clear teacher-student relationship: Musa (AS) as the teacher, and his young companion as the student. But now the roles are about to shift. The teacher is about to become the student. And remember what happened before? The student forgot certain instructions about the fish. Now watch what happens—the teacher who just dealt with his student’s forgetfulness is about to forget instructions himself. This is the elegant symmetry of the Quranic narrative.

Musa (AS) approaches with remarkable humility: هَلْ أَتَّبِعُكَ (Can I follow you?)—may I become your follower, your assistant? Who was following Musa (AS) around before? His fata, his young helper. Now Musa (AS) is asking, “Can I be your student? Do you have an internship available? Could I be your study assistant, your research assistant?”

عَلَىٰٓ أَن تُعَلِّمَنِ (On the condition that you teach me). Notice the humility in his language. He could have said تُعَلِّمُنِي, but instead he uses تُعَلِّمَنِ, a shorter, more humble form. It’s as if he’s saying, “Please teach me,” with genuine deference.

مِمَّا عُلِّمْتَ رُشْدًا (From what you have been taught, of right guidance). He acknowledges that this knowledge comes from Allah ﷻ—“from what you have been taught”—and he seeks rushd, sound guidance and wisdom.

Ayah 67

قَالَ إِنَّكَ لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِىَ صَبْرًا

The teacher’s response is immediate and firm: إِنَّكَ لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِىَ صَبْرًا (Indeed, you will never be able to have patience with me).

The emphasis is remarkable. He doesn’t just say لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ صَبْرًا (you won’t be able to be patient). He adds مَعِىَ (with me)—specifically with me. Perhaps you can be patient with other people in other situations, but you won’t be able to maintain patience with me. It seems this teacher already knows something about Musa (AS) that Allah ﷻ has made him aware of.
